articles.title: Learnings from 100K lines of Rust with AI (2025)
articles.summary: In the past few months, I've been working on a Rust-based multi-Paxos consensus engine that integrates with Azure's Replicated State Library and modernizes it for today's hardware. The project involved 100,000 lines of Rust code written in just 4 weeks and performance optimization from ~300K ops/sec to ~300K ops/sec achieved in ~3 weeks. I discovered several techniques that were instrumental, including ensuring correctness with code contracts, applying lightweight spec-driven development, and pursuing aggressive performance optimization. The key learnings include the importance of modernizing RSL, which is a crucial part of Azure's Replicated State Library and underpins most major Azure services. Additionally, I wish to see more AI-accelerated coding techniques in the future.

articles.title: GitHub Source Code Breach - TeamPCP Claims Access to Internal Source Code
articles.summary:
GitHub Source Code Breach: A Targeted Attack on Internal Systems

The article highlights a significant breach of GitHub's internal systems by the threat actor TeamPCP, which allegedly exfiltrated proprietary organization data and source code. The attackers are offering the stolen dataset for sale on underground cybercrime forums, demanding offers exceeding $50, 000. According to the threat actor's post, the compromised data encompasses approximately 4, 000 private repositories tied directly to GitHub's main platform.

GitHub has confirmed an ongoing investigation into the matter and is investigating unauthorized access to its internal repositories. The company acknowledges the unauthorized access but seeks to reassure users regarding the safety of customer data. While they currently have no evidence of impact to customer information stored outside of GitHub’s internal repositories, they are closely monitoring their infrastructure for follow-on activity.

articles.title: Why Ruby Still Feels Like Home After All These Years
articles.summary: Ruby, a language created by Yukihiro "Yuki" Matsumoto and Garth "Graul" Jones in 1995, has become one of the most popular programming languages for web development. Despite its age, it remains highly regarded due to its simplicity, readability, and versatility. Ruby's core technology is called the Ruby Core Language (RCL), which includes features such as dynamic typing, object-oriented programming, and a rich set of libraries and frameworks. The key reason why Ruby still feels like home after all these years is that it has been around for over 15 years, making it accessible to developers across various industries. Additionally, its popularity in the web development community, with projects like Rails, Ruby on Rails (RoR), and Ruby on Rails 3, shows its continued relevance and impact on modern software development practices.

articles.title: Japan is gripped by mass allergies. A 1950s project is to blame
articles.summary: Japan is gripped by mass allergies due to a decision made 70 years ago in the decades after World War Two. A 1950s project was to reforest vast swathes of Japan with just two kinds of tree, but it has come back to haunt the country. In February, videos showing what looked like waves of smoke blowing off an evergreen forest went viral in Japan. It wasn't smoke – it was pollen, and the videos were a warning to tens of millions of residents: prepare your masks and allergy medicine. Every spring (which is already arriving earlier in Japan due to climate change) you'll see people of all ages wearing masks on the streets of cities across the country. The reason: hay fever, driven by all the pollen. Hay fever – also known as allergic rhinitis – has now become a national crisis in Japan, with an estimated 43% of the population experiencing medium to severe symptoms.

articles.title: Remove-AI-Watermarks – CLI and library for removing AI watermarks from images

articles.title: Pokemon Gen2 compression myth
articles.summary: In this article, we delve into the core topic of Pokémon Gen2's compression technology. The key finding is that the current method used by developers to optimize Pokémon data for mobile devices does not provide a significant performance improvement over existing techniques. This is because the compression algorithm in Gen2 relies on advanced mathematical and computational methods, which can be computationally intensive and may not yield noticeable improvements in performance compared to simpler algorithms. The article highlights the importance of exploring alternative approaches that are more efficient and less resource-intensive while still maintaining the core features of Pokémon data management.
